extra.message: User input from request.form['expression'] (line 116) is passed directly to eval() at line 120. This allows arbitrary Python code execution, including os.system(), __import__('os').system('id'), or reading/writing files on the server.

User input from request.form['name'] (line 189) is embedded into a template string via %-formatting, then passed to render_template_string() at line 205. Jinja2 template syntax in user input (e.g. {{ config }}, {% for %}) will be evaluated server-side, allowing arbitrary code execution via SSTI.

User-supplied XML (from request.form['xml'] at line 156) is parsed with lxml.etree.XMLParser configured with no_network=False and dtd_validation=True. This enables DTD processing and external entity resolution, allowing XXE attacks that can read local files (e.g. /etc/passwd), perform SSRF, or cause denial of service via entity expansion.

The result of eval(expression) is concatenated directly into the HTML response string without escaping. If the expression returns a string containing HTML/JavaScript (e.g. '<script>alert(1)</script>'), it will execute in the victim's browser (reflected XSS).

The parsed XML output (parsed_xml) is concatenated directly into the HTML response string without escaping. If the XML contains HTML/JavaScript payloads (e.g. via DTD entity injection), they will execute in the victim's browser (reflected XSS).
The DNS lookup result is concatenated directly into the HTML response string without HTML escaping. Any HTML/JavaScript content in the output would execute in the victim's browser (reflected XSS).

The Flask app.secret_key is hardcoded as a plaintext string 'Harry Potter And The Deathly Hallows'. An attacker who knows this value can forge session cookies, impersonate any user, and potentially escalate privileges. Session cookies signed with this key can be forged.

Flask debug mode is enabled (app.debug = True). The Flask debugger allows arbitrary code execution via the debugger console at the /console endpoint if the debugger PIN is known or bypassed. Additionally, detailed stack traces are shown on errors, leaking source code, configuration, and internal paths.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The application binds to 0.0.0.0 (all network interfaces), making it accessible from any network. Combined with debug mode enabled, this exposes the debugger console to the network. The port is configured to 8000.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: User passwords are stored in plaintext in the database (User model line 26, registration at line 62). There is no hashing (e.g. bcrypt, scrypt, or pbkdf2) applied. If the database is compromised, all user passwords are immediately exposed in cleartext.

The 404 error handler passes the Flask error object to the template at error.html:20 which renders {{ error }}. When debug mode is enabled, the error object may contain sensitive stack trace information. Additionally, in debug mode, unhandled errors produce the Werkzeug debugger with a full stack trace and interactive console.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message

extra.message: The 'popen' function from os module is imported. On line 140, the undefined identifier 'rp(...)' is called instead of popen(), suggesting the intended code was command injection via popen('nslookup ' + user_input). While the NameError prevents exploitation in this exact version, this represents an intended command injection attack surface.
